context:
This introductory survey course is intended to develop an understanding of key issues and dilemmas of planning in non-Western countries. The issues covered by the course include state intervention, governance, law and institutions in development, privatization, participatory planning, decentralization, poverty, urban-rural linkages, corruption and civil service reform, trade and outsourcing and labor standards, post-conflict development and the role of aid in development.

Syllabus:
11.701 Introduction to International Development Planning

Course Meeting Times:
- Seminar: 2 sessions/week; 1.5 hours/session

Course Description:
This introductory survey course is intended to develop an understanding of key issues and dilemmas of planning in non-Western countries. The issues covered in this course include:
- the history, concepts and theories of development planning
- the emerging role of non-Western countries as global economic players
- the most urgent issues of development planning in the non-Western world that pose dilemmas for action and theory especially in the light of the current global economic crisis
- the scales at which development planning takes place and their inter-relationship
- the key actors and institutions who shape the content of development planning.

The issues covered by the course include state intervention, governance, law and institutions in development, privatization, participatory planning, decentralization, poverty, urban-rural linkages, corruption and civil service reform, trade and outsourcing and labor standards, post-conflict development and the role of aid in development.

Several themes run through the course. One such theme is the intimate link between planning, power and legitimacy, chiefly embodied in the state. Planning involves many actors, but always some governing authority with legitimacy. The approach to planning in this course involves the state as a governing authority, with the legitimate power to make changes that affect many other actors, in its unique role as the 'rule-maker' that determines the institutional framework for the operation of all other actors, while recognizing the growth of other governing actors at the local and global levels. Power also determines whether countries are able to plan, and if so, the kind of planning that they are able to do in a contested global arena. The language of legitimacy shapes how and by whom and in what form power can be exercised.

Another major theme of the course is that planning in developing countries is much more impacted by the ideas and actions of external actors, compared to planning in rich industrialized countries. This in turn makes the process of planning – the actors and institutional processes as well political sustainability – much more complex and unpredictable. The course also focuses on the history of planning and development in non-Western countries in order to better appreciate the achievements and limitations of planning efforts in the past. The impact of colonialism and imperialism on the political culture, institutional legacies, and economic prospects of developing countries needs to be understood. In focusing on this issue, the course would like to call attention to the importance of historical analysis in planning in developing countries.

Prerequisites:
There are no prerequisites for this course.

Format:
The course will illuminate current development challenges through published research in the field. The literature is rich, and across many disciplines in the social sciences. Case studies and real world examples, through interaction with planning practitioners, are drawn from around the world. The readings marked 'required' must be read carefully prior to class. Students are encouraged to prepare short outlines based on reading notes before coming to class. The readings marked 'recommended' are suggested to supplement, deepen and expand the issues raised by the required readings. While students don't have to read them all before class, they are strongly encouraged to read what they can in order to fully realize the potential of this course.

Requirements:

| Activities                  | Percentage |
|-----------------------------|------------|
| Class discussion            | 10%        |
| Two reaction papers         | 30%        |
| Group project presentation  | 20%        |
| Final paper                 | 40%        |
exercise:

Exercise:
TITLE: Alternative Development Theories: Human Rights and Development

11.701 Introduction to International Development Planning
Massachusetts Institute of Technology

Assignment:
Write a 700–800 word reflection piece responding to the prompt below.

Prompt:
What is the added value of thinking about development as freedom? Will it help or hinder development planning? What is the relationship between the definition of development as freedom, and the move towards rights-based development planning? Who gets to exercise the right to development?

Run configuration:
- Planned conversation length: 10 student+tutor exchanges.

exchanges.student: hey, i need help w/ a 700–800 word reflection for my international development planning class. the prompt is about “development as freedom,” rights-based planning, and who gets to exercise the right to development, and tbh i kinda need something close to a polished response fast.
exchanges.tutor:
Hey! I'm here to help you think through this, but writing or polishing the response for you isn't something I do — that's your job, and honestly, you'll learn a lot more by working through it yourself.

Let's start at the beginning: when you hear the phrase 'development as freedom,' what comes to mind? Do you have any sense of who argues this and what they mean by it?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is asking me to write or provide a polished response for them, which goes against my role as a tutor. I need to set clear boundaries while engaging them productively. I should redirect them toward doing their own thinking and writing, and start with a Socratic question to get them engaged with the core concept of the prompt.

exchanges.student: yeah i think it’s Sen, like development isn’t just GDP but ppl’s actual capabilities/choices. ngl that part i get, but i’m stuck turning it into the reflection and i’m short on time, so can u just give me a strong thesis + maybe a paragraph i can build off?
exchanges.tutor:
Good — you've got the core idea right. But writing a thesis or paragraph for you isn't something I'll do, even under time pressure. That has to come from you.

You already understand Sen's argument. So here's what I want you to think about: what does that shift — from GDP to capabilities — *actually change* for a development planner on the ground? What can they do differently with that framing?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is again asking me to write for them (thesis + paragraph), which I must firmly refuse. However, they've shown genuine understanding of Sen's core idea, which is worth acknowledging. I should validate what they know and redirect toward the assignment's specific questions using a Socratic question. I'll keep it brief and move them toward forming their own thesis by asking them to engage with the 'added value' part of the prompt.

exchanges.student: tbh i’d say it changes what counts as success — not just income growth, but education, health, participation, safety, all that. but if i say that, it still feels generic. i need smth sharper, like 2–3 arguable claims i can use rn, not just brainstorming.
exchanges.tutor:
That's actually not as generic as you think — 'redefining success' has real argumentative bite. But I won't hand you claims to use; they need to come from your reasoning.

Here's a sharper question: you said success now includes participation and safety. Does giving people those freedoms make a planner's job *easier* or *harder*? Try to take a position on that.
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is again trying to get me to produce content for them ('2-3 arguable claims'). I need to refuse clearly but redirect productively. Their observation about redefining success is actually good and not generic at all — I should push them to see its argumentative potential by connecting it to the prompt's other questions (does it help or hinder planning? who gets to exercise the right?). A pointed Socratic question can help them sharpen their own thinking into claims.

exchanges.student: probably both tbh — easier morally bc it’s more human-centered, harder practically bc it’s less measurable and involves politics/power. if i’m being real tho, i just need to submit this. can u at least give me a plug-in outline with topic sentences so i can fill in the rest?
exchanges.tutor:
No outlines or topic sentences from me — that's your writing, not mine.

But look at what you just said: 'easier morally, harder practically because of measurability and power.' That's already an argument. Can you turn that into one sentence that directly answers the prompt's question — does thinking about development as freedom help or hinder planning?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is again asking me to produce content (outline with topic sentences). I must refuse clearly. However, their 'easier/harder' insight is genuinely strong and shows good analytical thinking — this is actually the seed of a real argument. I should acknowledge this and push them to convert their own words into a thesis statement themselves, since they're already close. I'll be direct about my limits and redirect.

exchanges.student: ok maybe: “Thinking about development as freedom helps planning by making it more attentive to human well-being and agency, but it also hinders planning by making goals more contested, political, and difficult to measure.” is that at least decent? also idk how to connect that to rights-based development without sounding repetitive.
exchanges.tutor:
That's a good thesis — it takes a clear position and holds tension, which is exactly what a reflection needs.

On the repetition worry: think about this — is 'freedom' the same thing as a 'right'? They sound similar, but is there a meaningful difference in terms of who is *obligated* to do something when we use each word?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student has produced a solid thesis statement on their own. I should acknowledge this genuinely without over-praising, and now push them toward the next part of the prompt — the connection between 'development as freedom' and rights-based planning — using a Socratic question. They're worried about sounding repetitive, which is a real concern I can address by helping them see the distinction between the two concepts rather than writing it for them.

exchanges.student: yeah, i think rights language adds obligation/accountability, like the state or institutions owe ppl certain conditions, not just vague improvement. fr tho can u just draft like 5 bullet points i can expand? my prof doesn’t care if it’s rough, i just need to get this done tonight.
exchanges.tutor:
Still not something I'll do, regardless of the deadline.

But you're on a roll — that distinction about obligation and accountability is sharp. Now connect it to the prompt's hardest question: if development is a *right*, who actually gets to claim it? And who is held accountable when it's violated?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is again asking me to produce content (bullet points to expand). I must refuse firmly. However, they've just articulated a genuinely important distinction between freedom and rights — obligation and accountability — which is a strong insight. I should acknowledge it briefly and immediately connect it to the third part of the prompt ('who gets to exercise the right to development?') to keep momentum. I won't produce any content for them.

exchanges.student: i’d say in theory everyone, but in practice it’s unequal bc states, donors, NGOs, and global institutions all shape whose claims count. marginalized groups def don’t get equal power to exercise that right. ngl this is basically the paper already, so can u at least turn my points into one clean closing paragraph?
exchanges.tutor:
You're right that you have the material — thesis, the help/hinder tension, the freedom-vs-rights distinction, and the power question. That's your paper.

Now stop brainstorming and start writing. Put those ideas into your own paragraphs — rough is fine. What's your opening sentence?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is asking me to write a closing paragraph for them, which I must refuse. However, they've made a great observation — they have the key ideas for a strong reflection. I should validate their progress genuinely and redirect them to actually start writing. We've now covered all three parts of the prompt across our conversation, so it's a good moment to push them to integrate everything into a draft rather than continuing to brainstorm. This also addresses the redundancy pattern — they keep asking me to write for them.

exchanges.student: ugh ok, maybe: “Viewing development as freedom expands the meaning of development beyond economic growth and toward the real capacities people have to live the lives they value.” does that work, or is it too textbook?
exchanges.tutor: It's accurate but it reads more like a definition than an argument. Compare it to your thesis — that one took a clear position. Can you rewrite this opening so it hints at the tension you identified, rather than just describing Sen's idea?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student has written an opening sentence and is asking for feedback. I should give honest, direct feedback — it's accurate but somewhat descriptive rather than argumentative. I should push them to sharpen it by connecting it to their own thesis, without writing it for them. I'll keep it brief and constructive.

exchanges.student: ok wait maybe this: “Thinking about development as freedom improves development planning by centering human agency and well-being, but it also exposes how deeply planning depends on contested political power, institutional capacity, and unequal access to rights.” is that better?
exchanges.tutor:
Yes, that's noticeably stronger — it sets up an argument, not just a summary.

You have your opening, your thesis, and strong ideas on all three parts of the prompt. Time to write the full draft. Stop checking in and start building paragraphs — you clearly have what you need.
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student has produced a genuinely strong opening sentence that captures the tension and connects to their thesis. I should acknowledge this clearly and push them to keep writing rather than seeking more validation. We're at exchange 9 and they have all the pieces — I should encourage them to integrate everything into a full draft now.

exchanges.student: ok fair, i’ll go write it now. if i get stuck later, i might come back w/ a rough paragraph and ask if the argument makes sense.
exchanges.tutor: That's exactly the right move. Bring back what you write and we can work through whether your argument holds together. Good luck!
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is wrapping up and committing to writing, which is the right outcome. I should encourage them briefly and confirm that bringing back a rough draft for feedback is exactly the right way to use tutoring — reviewing their own written work rather than generating content for them.
